title: Transformer Micro-Architecture: RMSNorm, SwiGLU, RoPE & ALiBi
why_next: You serve these architectures daily but the specific design choices — why RMSNorm over LayerNorm, why SwiGLU over GeLU, why RoPE beats learned position embeddings — affect inference speed, quantization behaviour, and fine-tuning stability.
concepts: RoPE rotation math vs ALiBi linear bias

title: FastAPI Internals: Dependency Injection, Background Tasks & Lifespan
why_next: You use FastAPI daily but mostly the surface. The DI resolution graph, sub-dependencies, yield-based teardown, lifespan vs startup events, and BackgroundTasks vs proper task queues are the patterns that keep a large service maintainable.
concepts: dependency resolution graph & caching per request
lifespan context vs deprecated startup/shutdown events
scoped dependencies & dependency overrides for testing

title: Tokenization Deep Dive: BPE, Unigram LM & Token Budget Effects
why_next: Tokenization choices affect inference throughput, your RAG chunking strategy, and fine-tuning data quality. Understanding BPE merge rules, unigram LM, and byte-level fallbacks lets you debug token budget explosions and pick chunking boundaries correctly.
concepts: token budget calculation for chunking & context windows

title: REST API Design, Versioning & Backward Compatibility
why_next: Clean API surfaces outlive their implementations. Resource modeling, idempotency, pagination, error contracts, and versioning strategy (URI vs header vs content negotiation) determine whether clients break on every change.

title: Embedding Models & Contrastive Training
why_next: You run hybrid RAG with off-the-shelf embeddings. Understanding InfoNCE, hard-negative mining, and Matryoshka embeddings lets you fine-tune a retriever for your domain data — the biggest quality lever you haven't pulled yet.

title: PostgreSQL Query Planner, Indexing & EXPLAIN ANALYZE
why_next: You use PostgreSQL as a black box. Reading EXPLAIN ANALYZE, understanding the planner's cost model, and choosing B-tree vs GIN vs BRIN vs partial indexes is the single highest-leverage skill for backend performance.
concepts: B-tree vs GIN vs GiST vs BRIN vs partial indexes

title: Constrained Decoding & Grammar-Based Generation
why_next: Your agents need reliable JSON and tool-call outputs. outlines/XGrammar compile JSON schemas to FSMs that mask logits each step — turning flaky parsing into guaranteed-valid output, and slotting directly into your sampling loop.

title: Redis Internals: Persistence, Clustering & Data Structures
why_next: You use Redis for caching and pub/sub. Go deeper: RDB vs AOF durability, the single-threaded event loop, Streams for event sourcing, Lua scripting for atomicity, and Cluster hash-slot sharding — the patterns that make Redis carry real load.
concepts: RDB snapshot vs AOF persistence & fsync policies
Lua scripting for atomic multi-key ops
Cluster hash slots, resharding & hot-key mitigation

title: Sampling & Decoding Strategies: Temperature, Top-p, Min-p & Beam
why_next: You tune temperature and top-p by feel. The math behind nucleus, top-k, min-p, typical sampling, and beam search determines output diversity, repetition, and latency — and interacts directly with the constrained decoding and speculative work in your engine.

title: Rate Limiting, Throttling & Backpressure
why_next: Every public service needs protection from overload. Token bucket vs sliding window vs leaky bucket, distributed rate limiting in Redis, and backpressure/load shedding are the controls that keep a system alive under a traffic spike.
concepts: token bucket vs sliding window vs leaky bucket
distributed rate limiting with Redis + Lua

title: KV Cache Quantization & Eviction Policies
why_next: Your PagedAttention engine manages KV blocks. The next frontier is shrinking them: KIVI per-channel quant, H2O heavy-hitter eviction, StreamingLLM attention sinks — all slot into your block allocator.

title: Circuit Breakers, Bulkheads, Retries & Timeouts
why_next: When a downstream dependency is slow or failing, a naive retry storm amplifies the problem. Circuit breakers, bulkhead isolation, exponential backoff with jitter, hedged requests, and deadline propagation keep cascading failures contained.

title: Quantization Deep Dive: GPTQ, AWQ, FP8
why_next: You deploy quantized models via vLLM/SGLang. Go a layer down: GPTQ's Hessian-based rounding, AWQ's activation-aware scaling, FP8 E4M3 vs E5M2 — so you can reason about when to use each and what quality you're trading away.

title: gRPC & Protobuf: Schema Design and Streaming
why_next: You use REST everywhere. gRPC's proto3 schema-first contracts, HTTP/2 multiplexing, and bidirectional streaming are strictly better for internal service-to-service calls and streaming workloads where REST starts to show seams.
concepts: unary vs server / client / bidi streaming

title: Speculative Decoding & Draft Models
why_next: You built continuous batching + PagedAttention from scratch. Speculative decoding is the single biggest latency win left and slots directly into your autoregressive verify loop.

title: Database Transactions: Isolation Levels & Concurrency Control
why_next: Correctness under concurrency is where most data bugs hide. Read-committed vs repeatable-read vs serializable, the anomalies each prevents, MVCC vs locking, and optimistic vs pessimistic concurrency decide whether your money/inventory logic is sound.
concepts: ANSI isolation levels & the anomalies they prevent

title: Mixture-of-Experts: Routing, Load Balancing & Capacity
why_next: DeepSeek, Mixtral, and most frontier models are MoE. Top-k gating, expert parallelism, capacity factor, and token dropping are fundamentally different from dense serving — and your vLLM setup needs to know this.

title: LLD: SOLID, Design Patterns & Clean Interfaces
why_next: Your services grow messy without disciplined low-level design. SOLID, dependency inversion, and the factory/strategy/observer/adapter patterns are the vocabulary for decoupling modules so they stay testable and changeable.

title: Long-Context Attention: RoPE Scaling, Ring Attention & Sparse
why_next: Your RAG system fights context limits constantly. YaRN/NTK RoPE scaling, ring attention for million-token context, and sparse patterns let you reason about when to extend context vs retrieve — and the quality tradeoffs of each.

title: Kafka: Partitioning, Consumer Groups & Exactly-Once Semantics
why_next: Event-driven backbones decouple producers from consumers and enable replay. Partition assignment, consumer-group rebalancing, the idempotent-producer + transaction machinery behind exactly-once, and lag monitoring are the core of stream processing.

title: RLHF, DPO & Preference Alignment
why_next: You've done SFT-style LoRA. DPO — the cleaner successor to PPO-RLHF — is the natural next training skill. It also clarifies how the models you serve were aligned and why they behave under adversarial prompts.

title: Consistent Hashing & Distributed Hash Tables
why_next: Consistent hashing is the primitive behind sharded caches, partitioned databases, and load balancers. Virtual nodes, bounded-load variants, and rendezvous hashing determine how your system rebalances when nodes join or fail.

title: PEFT Beyond LoRA: QLoRA, DoRA, LoRA-FA & Adapter Merging
why_next: You know LoRA. QLoRA's NF4 + double quantization, DoRA's magnitude/direction decomposition, and adapter merging unlock fine-tuning larger models on the same hardware and serving multiple adapters on one base model.

title: CAP, PACELC & Consistency Models
why_next: Every distributed datastore choice is a consistency tradeoff. CAP and PACELC, the spectrum from linearizable to eventual to causal consistency, and quorum (R+W>N) math are the framework for reasoning about what your system actually guarantees.
concepts: linearizable vs sequential vs causal vs eventual

title: Scaling Laws: Chinchilla, Compute-Optimal Training & Data Quality
why_next: You fine-tune models but probably pick them by benchmark. Scaling laws tell you the exact compute-optimal (N, D) pair for a FLOP budget, and Chinchilla revised the prior that bigger is always better.
concepts: compute-optimal N and D for a fixed C

title: Database Sharding & Replication Strategies
why_next: Scaling a database past one node forces hard choices. Range vs hash vs directory sharding, sync vs async replication, leader election, failover, and the resharding problem are what separate a prototype DB layer from one that survives growth.
concepts: range vs hash vs directory-based sharding

title: Synthetic Data Generation & Knowledge Distillation
why_next: You've done SFT-style fine-tuning. Distillation (teacher logits → student), self-instruct generation, rejection sampling, and evol-instruct are the data-engine skills behind every high-quality fine-tune — directly applicable to your uCube.ai work.
concepts: data quality scoring with reward models

title: Event Sourcing & CQRS
why_next: Storing state as an immutable event log instead of mutable rows gives you audit, replay, and temporal queries for free. CQRS splits the write and read models — powerful, but it introduces eventual consistency and projection-rebuild complexity you must design for.
concepts: event log as source of truth

title: Multimodal & Vision-Language Model Architecture
why_next: A genuine gap in your stack. How VLMs fuse vision encoders (CLIP/SigLIP) with LLMs via projectors, image tokenization costs, high-res tiling, and how multimodal inputs change your serving assumptions and KV cache sizing.

title: Saga Pattern & Distributed Transactions
why_next: You can't run a 2PC across microservices in practice. Sagas coordinate multi-service workflows with compensating transactions — orchestration vs choreography, the outbox pattern, and idempotency are how you keep distributed state consistent without locks.

title: Model Merging: Task Arithmetic, TIES, DARE & SLERP
why_next: Model merging combines multiple LoRA-fine-tuned checkpoints into one multi-skill model without retraining. This builds on your LoRA work and gives you a cheap path to multi-capability models for different uCube.ai use cases.

title: CRDTs & Conflict Resolution in Distributed Systems
why_next: When multiple nodes accept writes, conflicts are inevitable. CRDTs (G-counters, OR-sets, LWW-registers) give mathematically guaranteed convergence without coordination — the foundation of collaborative and offline-first systems.

title: LLM Uncertainty, Calibration & Conformal Prediction
why_next: Your RAG system returns answers with no confidence signal. Calibrated uncertainty — semantic entropy, conformal prediction intervals, logit-based confidence — lets you route uncertain queries to fallbacks, flag for human review, or escalate to a larger model.

title: Observability: Distributed Tracing, Metrics & Logs with OpenTelemetry
why_next: A distributed system you can't observe is one you can't operate. OpenTelemetry traces/spans, context propagation across service boundaries, RED/USE metrics, and correlating logs-metrics-traces are what make incidents debuggable.

title: Constitutional AI & RLAIF: Scalable Alignment Pipelines
why_next: Constitutional AI and RLAIF remove the human labeler bottleneck from alignment. Understanding how AI-generated preference data and principle-guided critique chains work lets you design alignment pipelines for domain-specific models.

title: API Gateway & Backend-for-Frontend Design
why_next: As services multiply, clients shouldn't talk to each one directly. An API gateway centralizes auth, routing, rate limiting, and aggregation; the BFF pattern tailors the surface per client — the seam where cross-cutting concerns live.
concepts: gateway as a single point of failure & mitigation

title: Pre-Training Data Curation: Quality Signals, Dedup & Domain Mix
why_next: You fine-tune on top of pre-trained models but base quality is set by pre-training data. Understanding the signals (perplexity, reward scoring, domain mix) that drive base quality helps you choose and evaluate foundation models better.
concepts: global dedup with MinHash at billion-doc scale

title: Microservices Decomposition with DDD Bounded Contexts
why_next: Splitting a monolith wrong creates a distributed monolith — worse than what you started with. DDD bounded contexts, aggregate boundaries, inter-service contracts, and the data-ownership rule guide where to actually draw service lines.

title: Embedding & Retrieval Evaluation: nDCG, MRR & Calibration
why_next: You ship retrieval but measure it informally. The math of nDCG, MRR, Recall@K, plus building gold sets and detecting embedding drift, turns retrieval tuning from guesswork into a measurable optimization loop.
concepts: offline metric vs online quality correlation

title: Zero-Downtime Deployments: Blue-Green, Canary & Rolling
why_next: Shipping without dropping requests requires deliberate traffic management. Blue-green swaps, canary with progressive rollout, rolling updates, plus the hard part — backward-compatible schema migrations and feature flags — are how mature teams deploy continuously.

title: FlashAttention 2/3 Internals & IO-Aware Tiling
why_next: You use FlashAttention as a black box. Understanding online softmax, SRAM tiling, and FA3's warp specialization is the prerequisite for writing your own fused kernels and reasoning about memory-bound bottlenecks.

title: Kubernetes: Scheduling, Resource Limits & HPA/VPA
why_next: Running services on Kubernetes well means understanding the scheduler. Requests vs limits, QoS classes, affinity/taints, and HPA/VPA/cluster-autoscaler decide whether your pods get placed, throttled, or OOM-killed under load.

title: Attention Variants: GQA, MQA & Multi-Head Latent Attention
why_next: These variants directly shrink your KV cache. GQA/MQA key-value grouping and DeepSeek's MLA latent compression are the architectural counterpart to your cache management work — knowing the math lets you calculate actual memory savings.
concepts: MHA vs MQA vs GQA KV sharing math
KV cache size formula per variant

title: Service Mesh: Istio, Envoy & the Sidecar Pattern
why_next: Once you run many services, re-implementing mTLS, retries, and circuit breaking in each one is wasteful. A service mesh pushes these into a sidecar proxy at the infra layer — with its own latency, complexity, and failure-mode costs to weigh.
concepts: sidecar proxy (Envoy) data plane vs control plane

title: Reasoning Models: Long-CoT Scaling, MCTS & Process Reward Models
why_next: o1/R1-style models are the frontier of what you serve. Process vs outcome reward models, MCTS for trace generation, and why long CoT at inference trades compute for accuracy reshapes how you think about serving cost.
concepts: serving cost of long-CoT models vs dense models

title: SLO, SLA, Error Budgets & Capacity Planning
why_next: Reliability is an engineering target, not a vibe. Defining SLIs, deriving SLOs from user journeys, spending error budgets to balance velocity vs stability, and capacity planning from load tests turn 'is it healthy?' into a number.
concepts: SLI selection & SLO targets from user journeys

title: Mechanistic Interpretability: Circuits, Superposition & Activation Patching
why_next: As you build systems that depend on model behavior, understanding why models do what they do — induction heads, superposition of features, activation patching for causal tracing — lets you debug unexpected behaviors and reason about capability edges.
concepts: superposition of features in residual stream

title: Caching Strategies & Invalidation at Scale
why_next: Caching is the easiest performance win and the easiest correctness footgun. Cache-aside vs write-through vs write-behind, TTL/stampede control, multi-tier caches, and the consistency model of each are what keep a hot path fast without serving stale data.
concepts: cache stampede: locking, request coalescing, early refresh

title: Knowledge Editing: ROME, MEMIT & Locate-and-Edit
why_next: When a fact in your model changes, you choose between full re-train and surgical knowledge editing. ROME/MEMIT locate the factual association in MLP layers and update it directly — understanding this tells you when editing works and when it breaks.
concepts: causal tracing to locate factual associations
when editing beats fine-tuning (and when it doesn't)

title: Message Queues: Delivery Semantics, DLQs & Idempotency
why_next: Queues glue async systems together but at-least-once delivery means duplicates are guaranteed. Dead-letter queues, visibility timeouts, idempotent consumers, ordering guarantees, and poison-message handling are what make async reliable.

title: State-Space Models: Mamba, Selective Scan & Hybrid Architectures
why_next: Mamba-2 and hybrid Mamba-transformer models are entering production. Linear-time context scaling changes your serving math: O(1) per-token inference, no KV cache to manage, but fundamentally different throughput/latency curves than transformers.
concepts: linear vs quadratic context scaling tradeoff

title: HLD: Design a Twitter/News-Feed System
why_next: The canonical scale-system interview and a real design problem: fan-out-on-write vs fan-out-on-read, the celebrity hot-key problem, timeline caching, and how to estimate QPS, storage, and bandwidth from first principles.

title: Tensor, Pipeline & Sequence Parallelism
why_next: Single-GPU inference is solved. Multi-GPU/multi-node serving requires understanding how Megatron-style TP splits matmuls across GPUs and how PP schedules micro-batches — essential for models that don't fit on one GPU.

title: Consensus: Raft, Leader Election & Replicated Logs
why_next: Behind every strongly-consistent distributed system is a consensus protocol. Raft's leader election, log replication, and commit rules — plus why split-brain happens and how quorums prevent it — are the foundation of etcd, Consul, and Kafka's controller.
concepts: Raft vs Paxos vs ZAB tradeoffs

title: Distributed Training: FSDP, ZeRO & Gradient Sharding
why_next: You fine-tune with LoRA on a single GPU. FSDP/ZeRO sharding (params, gradients, optimizer state) unlocks full fine-tunes and larger models — the training-side analog of your serving parallelism work.

title: HLD: Design Uber/Ride-Matching with Geospatial Indexing
why_next: A different scaling axis — real-time geospatial. Quadtrees/geohash/H3 indexing, proximity matching at scale, dispatch consistency, and surge pricing pipelines stress location-aware sharding and low-latency matching in ways feed systems don't.
concepts: location update ingestion at high write rate
surge pricing as a streaming aggregation

title: GPU Profiling & Roofline Analysis
why_next: You optimize inference by intuition. Nsight/torch profiler, roofline modeling (arithmetic intensity vs memory bandwidth), and identifying memory-bound vs compute-bound ops give you a rigorous methodology instead of guesswork.

title: HLD: Design YouTube/Video Streaming & CDN
why_next: Storage- and bandwidth-dominated design. Chunked upload, transcoding pipelines, adaptive bitrate (HLS/DASH), CDN edge caching, and the metadata vs blob split push you to reason about petabyte-scale storage and global delivery.
concepts: metadata store vs blob store split

title: Writing Fused Kernels with Triton
why_next: Triton lets you write fused ops (fused RMSNorm+linear, fused dequant+GEMM) without raw CUDA — extending your inference engine into custom-kernel territory and closing the last performance gap vs hand-tuned CUDA.

title: Capstone: Design a Production-Grade Distributed Backend
why_next: The synthesis of the whole track. Design a complete multi-service backend end to end: API gateway, sharded data layer, event backbone, consistency model, deployment strategy, and SLO-driven observability — every decision justified with numbers and failure analysis.
concepts: SLO derivation & capacity planning under load
evolution path from monolith MVP to scale
